attributes.stem:
A 77-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with pain in his left foot. He initially experienced calf pain on walking, but over the past month has developed constant foot pain at night that is only relieved by hanging his foot over the edge of the bed. He has a medical history of hypertension and ischaemic heart disease. His medications include perindopril and atorvastatin, though he admits poor compliance.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 85/min, blood pressure 155/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. The left leg is cool and pale with a diminished femoral pulse and absent distal pulses. The right leg has normal pulses throughout.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following features would most strongly indicate the need for urgent revascularisation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features of chronic limb-threatening ischaemia (CLTI), characterised by rest pain relieved by dependency. The presence of motor deficit indicates severe, potentially irreversible ischaemia requiring immediate intervention.

Loss of toe movement or motor strength is the most concerning feature as it indicates severe muscle ischaemia that may lead to irreversible damage and limb loss if not promptly treated. This finding necessitates urgent vascular surgery review for consideration of revascularisation.

Venous guttering occurs when superficial veins collapse due to reduced arterial inflow. While this indicates significant arterial disease, it is not as concerning as motor deficit and can be present in less severe disease.

Temperature difference between limbs is a common finding in arterial insufficiency. While it helps confirm the diagnosis, it does not necessarily indicate urgent intervention is needed.

Reduced sensation in the foot can occur due to ischaemic neuropathy or coexisting conditions like diabetes. While concerning, it is less specific for immediate threat to limb viability than motor loss.

Dusky discolouration of toes can occur in various conditions including cholesterol embolisation (blue toe syndrome) and is not specific for severe limb-threatening ischaemia requiring immediate intervention.

The management of CLTI involves urgent vascular imaging (typically CT angiography) followed by revascularisation through either endovascular or open surgical approaches. Risk factor modification including smoking cessation, antiplatelet therapy, and optimal medical management of cardiovascular comorbidities is also essential.
learningPoints: Loss of motor function in chronic limb-threatening ischaemia indicates an immediately threatened limb requiring urgent revascularisation.
Rest pain relieved by leg dependency is a cardinal feature of chronic limb-threatening ischaemia.
Chronic limb-threatening ischaemia requires at least two weeks of rest pain, ulceration, or gangrene to meet diagnostic criteria.
references: Neschis DG, Golden MA. Clinical features and diagnosis of lower extremity peripheral artery disease. In Uptodate [Online]. 2021; cited 2021 Sep 23. Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/clinical-features-and-diagnosis-of-lower-extremity-peripheral-artery-disease
Farber A. Chronic limb-threatening ischemia. N Engl J Med. 2018;379(2):171-80. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29996085/

Cumming M. Clinical Evaluation for Peripheral Arterial Disease [Online]. 2021; cited 2021 Sep 23. Available at: https://www.drcumming.com/educational-musings/clinical-evaluation-for-peripheral-arterial-disease

Saric M. Embolism from atherosclerotic plaque: Atheroembolism (cholesterol crystal embolism). In: Uptodate [Online]. 2021; cited 2021 Sep 23. Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/embolism-from-atherosclerotic-plaque-atheroembolism-cholesterol-crystal-embolism

Mitchell ME, Carpenter JP. Clinical features and diagnosis of acute lower extremity ischaemia. In: Uptodate [Online]. 2021; cited 2021 Sep 23. Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/clinical-features-and-diagnosis-of-acute-lower-extremity-ischemia
